1:
The chapter opens with a king taking counsel, and the witnesses gather first around the political and religious weight of that consultation. The reformed John Gill fixes the occasion: David consulted with the chiliarchs and centurions, with every officer of the great armed body that had met in every tribe to make and crown him king, so that the advice was taken at the coronation itself, though (as Dr. Lightfoot observed) the thing resolved was not executed until some considerable time afterward — between the counsel and the deed fell Hiram’s embassy, the taking of Jerusalem, and the two battles with the Philistines. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the same scene in its logic: having seized the stronghold of Zion, organised his army, and secured the friendly disposition of Israel toward him, David judged it right to do what he could for the establishment of religion, and as a first step consulted on the propriety of bringing the ark from an obscure village to the royal city.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dates the meeting after the coronation at Hebron and, comparing 2 Samuel 6:1, resists the notion that this assembly is still the great muster of warriors at Hebron; Samuel implies otherwise. He parses the offices with care: the “captains of thousands” are the commanders of the clans, the “hundreds” the smaller divisions of the houses (comparing Numbers 31:14), and “every leader” is rather every prince or nagid — the chiefs who together constituted the Great Council of the nation. His fellow grammatical-historical witnesses Keil and Delitzsch stand back to survey the whole design, observing that where the author of Samuel treated the removal of the ark in 2 Samuel 6 chiefly for its political bearing on David’s kingship, the Chronicler’s governing interest is religious — to display David’s purpose of restoring the Levitic worship prescribed in the Mosaic law, the account of the conquest of Zion standing just before at 1 Chron. 11:4-8.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the consultation into a pattern of pious rule. As soon as David was well seated on his throne he had thoughts of the ark: not “What pompous thing shall I do now?” nor “What pleasant thing?” but “What pious thing?” — for it is the wisdom of those who are enriched and preferred to honour God with their honours, taking his oracles for their counsellors. And though as king he might have commanded, David chose to consult, showing respect to the great men and making the work a national act that it might procure a national blessing: “in the multitude of counsellors there is safety.” Henry marks his humility in calling the people brethren, and his honesty in owning the neglect of Saul’s days — “we enquired not at it” — making no peevish reflection on the dead king but taking the guilt to himself. The witnesses do not divide; together they frame the consultation as the first act of a reign that would set the worship of God at its centre.
2:
“And David said unto all the congregation of Israel” — the witnesses attend to the form of the appeal and to the body that received it. The reformed John Gill notes simply that David addressed the assembled officers as the representatives of the whole nation. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add that the congregation approved the design, and that when David then gathered “all Israel” the phrase reaches from the southern frontier of Canaan to the northern, though not all were present in person; there were numerous representatives, a chosen number of thirty thousand men according to 2 Samuel 6:1.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, works over the syntax of the king’s proposal. “And that it be” is better “and if it be,” the clause standing independent, so that David says in effect, “If before you the thing be good, and if the motion come from Jehovah” — the former turn of phrase a late-Hebrew idiom recurring in Nehemiah 2:5, the latter illustrated by Genesis 24:50. “Let us send abroad” is literally “let us break forth, let us send,” that is, send with all despatch; “everywhere” is not in the Hebrew; and “land” is rather “lands” or territories, the plural implying the partition of Canaan among the tribes, as in Genesis 26:3-4. The priests and Levites are summoned in “the cities of their pastures,” for the Levites busied themselves with pastoral pursuits when not engaged in the service of religion (compare 1 Chron. 6:57); the whole invitation was a kind of formal proclamation to the entire people of a solemn act of national importance.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley supplies the narrative frame the bare verse omits. After the proposal was made by the king and accepted by the people, he says, the great assembly was dismissed, some being kept back to go with David against Jerusalem; but before the resolution could be carried out the Philistines came and fought with David twice, as related in 2 Samuel 5:17 and following and again in 1 Chron. 14:8, and only after they were repulsed did David take up the matter again and call another general assembly. Wesley reads “that it be of the LORD” as “if this translation of the ark be pleasing to God,” and hears in “our brethren that are left” the memory of the great desolations God had made among them for their sins. The witnesses do not contend; they show a king who will not move on so weighty a matter without the concurrence of his people and the approval of his God.
3:
“And let us bring again the ark of our God to us: for we enquired not at it in the days of Saul.” Here the witnesses weigh both the longing of the king and the charge of past neglect. The reformed John Gill marks the ark as the symbol of the divine Presence, than which nothing was more desirable to David, who chose to begin his reign over all Israel with it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders “bring again” as “bring round,” the transfer of the ark from Kirjath-jearim to Jerusalem answering to the transfer of the throne from Saul to David (he compares 1 Chron. 10:14 and 1 Chron. 12:23). The ark had lain in the house of Abinadab at Kirjath-jearim, a city of David’s own tribe, for twenty years after the Philistines sent it back, as 1 Samuel 7:2 records; now the king would make it the centre of the national worship in his new capital.

On the clause “we enquired not at it” Ellicott presses a nicety that touches the whole theology of the ark. The verb means rather “we sought it not” — that is, neglected it, cared nothing for it; and there may be a reference to Saul’s despairing neglect of consulting the LORD in 1 Chron. 10:13. Indeed he suggests we should perhaps read “we sought Him not,” referring the pronoun to God (comparing 1 Chron. 15:13 and Isaiah 9:12), for there is no clear evidence that the ark itself was ever used as an oracle — the mercy-seat of Exodus 25:10-22 was the place of meeting, yet by Solomon’s day nothing was in the ark save the two tables of stone, as 1 Kings 8:9 records.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fills in the state of things the verse laments. The ark was then neglected, and the generality of the people contented themselves with going to Gibeon to offer sacrifices there, not caring that the ark — the very soul of the tabernacle — was in another place. As soon as David had power in his hand, Wesley observes, he would use it for the advancement of religion; for it ought to be the first care of those who are enriched or preferred to honour God with their honours, and to serve him and the interests of his kingdom with their wealth and power. The witnesses converge: the removal of the ark is at once the satisfying of David’s own devout desire and the deliberate mending of a long, guilty inattention that had marked the previous reign.
4:
“And all the congregation said that they would do so: for the thing was right in the eyes of all the people.” The verse records the people’s assent, and the witnesses, though brief, do not let its unanimity pass unweighed. The reformed John Gill notes only that they agreed to the motion with one mind. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the reply “So should we do,” comparing the construction at 1 Chron. 5:5 and 1 Chron. 9:25, and glosses “the thing” as the king’s proposal — the whole design of fetching up the ark.

Around that plain concurrence the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the proposal, builds his fullest reflection. The people’s ready agreement, he observes, was such that nobody could say a word to the contrary; it was a very good work and very seasonable, and so it was resolved nemine contradicente — unanimously. Henry draws two lessons from that quickness of consent. The first is an encouragement to those who lead in good works: they that prudently propose a good thing, and set themselves at the head of it, will perhaps find a more ready concurrence than they expected, for great men know not what a great deal of good they are capable of doing by their influence upon others. The second is the securing of the work by the people’s own act: by taking their representatives along in the resolve, David makes the removal of the ark a national undertaking, so that it might the more surely draw down a national blessing.

The witnesses do not divide; the harmony of the verse is itself their theme. What David had submitted to the twofold test of the previous verses — whether it seemed good to the assembly and whether it was of the LORD — the assembly now answers on the first count with a single voice, and the historian is careful to say not merely that the leaders agreed but that “the thing was right in the eyes of all the people,” the consent running down from the council of chiefs to the whole body of Israel. Here is the picture the Chronicler wishes to leave: not a king imposing a cult by decree, but a nation moved as one to seek again the neglected token of God’s presence. The very readiness of the answer measures how deeply the ark’s long exile at Kirjath-jearim had been felt, and how welcome its restoration would be — a welcome that makes all the more startling the disaster which the next verses, at the threshing-floor, will bring upon this joyful and united enterprise.
5:
“So David gathered all Israel together, from Shihor of Egypt even unto the entering of Hemath, to bring the ark of God from Kirjath-jearim.” The witnesses spread out the map of the gathering and dispute its southern landmark. The reformed John Gill takes “all Israel” of the principal men, the thirty thousand chosen. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch narrate the whole procession in one sweep — David and all Israel going up to Baalah, drawing the ark on a new cart from the house of Abinadab, and playing before God with song and every instrument until the disaster at the threshing-floor — reading the muster “from Shihor even unto Hemath” as marking the two frontiers, south and north, of the land whose representatives came up.

Over the word “Shihor” the witnesses genuinely differ. Charles Ellicott identifies it with the boundary between Egypt and Canaan, the winter torrent elsewhere called the River of Egypt (he cites Isaiah 27:12 and 2 Chron. 7:8), the modern Wady el Arish, which Joshua 13:3 also names Shihor or “Blackwater” — though he grants that in Isaiah 23:3 the same word means the Nile. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes that other road, following the Targum, which renders the phrase “from the Nile of Egypt even to the entrance of Antioch.” So the southern marker is read either as the little border-wady of the Sinai frontier or as the great river of Egypt itself; the difference measures the whole breadth ascribed to David’s summons.

On the northern landmark the witnesses agree. Ellicott notes that “the entering of Hemath” — Hamath — was the usual designation of the north boundary of Palestine, as the torrent of Egypt was of the south; Hamath was the seat of an ancient kingdom friendly to David, called by Amos “Great Hamath” in Amos 6:2. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the devotional sense from the very sweep of the geography: it was worth travelling far to attend the ark of God, and the people came from the utmost south to the furthest north to grace the solemnity. We have reason, he says, greatly to rejoice in the revival of neglected ordinances and the return of the tokens of God’s presence; when religion, long hidden in obscurity, is openly professed and countenanced by princes and great ones, it is a happy omen worthy to be welcomed with joy. Yet Henry adds the sobering note the sequel will justify — that when, after long disuse, ordinances come to be revived, it is too common for even wise and good men to fall into some mistake.
9:
“And when they came unto the threshing-floor of Chidon, Uzza put forth his hand to hold the ark; for the oxen stumbled.” The joy of the procession breaks here, and the witnesses fasten on the rash hand and on the name of the place. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sifts the text: “Chidon” is the “Nachon” of Samuel, which he thinks the right form, one Greek manuscript reading Chidon and the Syriac and Arabic “Ramin,” while the Targum and Syriac of Samuel treat the word as a participle and render “the prepared threshing-floor.” “Put forth his hand to hold the ark” is an explanatory paraphrase of the older and barer text of Samuel, “Uzza put forth unto the ark of God, and held thereon.” And “the oxen stumbled” he would render “the oxen plunged” — the verb is used transitively, of throwing down, in 2 Kings 9:33, where Jezebel is cast from the window — so that the beasts did not merely trip but lurched violently, and Uzza’s hand shot out in the instinct of the moment.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes quickly over the event, referring the reader to his fuller treatment of the same transaction at 2 Samuel 6:6. It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who dwells on the sin itself. Let the sin of Uzza, he says, warn us all to take heed of presumption, rashness, and irreverence in dealing about holy things, and not to think that a good intention will justify a bad action. In our communion with God we must watch over our own hearts, lest familiarity breed contempt and we come to imagine that God is somehow beholden to us. Uzza meant well — he put out his hand to save the ark from what looked like a fall — and yet the meaning well did not excuse the touching of what he had no right to touch.

The witnesses do not divide, but between them they locate the tragedy at two depths. The immediate fault is Uzza’s own: a hasty, over-familiar hand laid on the sacred ark. But Henry’s word about “walking by rule” at the cart has already reached forward to this moment, for it was the cart itself — the ark swaying on wheels behind plunging oxen instead of riding steady on the Levites’ shoulders — that put Uzza in the very position where his instinct would betray him. The presumption of the hand and the irregularity of the method meet at the threshing-floor of Chidon, and the reader who has followed the witnesses sees that the disaster, when it comes in the next verse, is no bolt from a clear sky but the ripening of a reverent carelessness.
6:
“And David went up, and all Israel, to Baalah, that is, to Kirjath-jearim... to bring up thence the ark of God the LORD, that dwelleth between the cherubims, whose name is called on it.” From this point, Charles Ellicott observes, the narrative runs parallel with 2 Samuel 6:2 and following, the two texts plainly springing from one original, the Chronicler’s differences being abridgments, paraphrases, and corrections. The grammatical-historical witness identifies the place: “Baalah,” or “Baal’s town,” is the Kirjath-baal of Joshua 15:60, another name for Kirjath-jearim, “the town of woods”; and here, he judges, the Chronicle’s text stands more correct than that of Samuel. He would render the great title of the ark “Jehovah, who sitteth upon the cherubim” (comparing Psalms 80:2 and Isaiah 37:16), the ark being the throne of the invisible King; and Keil and Delitzsch carry the same verse within their running narrative of the ascent.

The crux is the closing phrase, “whose name is called on it.” The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, again with the Targum, reads it “where his name is invoked,” and insists on the preposition: the Hebrew does not say the Name was called upon the ark, but invoked at it. Ellicott approaches the same words through the later Jewish reverence for the divine Name: the Hebrew reads “who is called Name,” for the Israelites of after days shrank from all mention of Jehovah and put in its place “the Name” — hashshem — a scruple he traces to Leviticus 24:16 and the third commandment. Comparing 2 Samuel 6:2, he suspects a small word meaning “upon it” has dropped from the text, so that the sense is “the ark upon which the Name of Jehovah is called” — the ark that bears God’s own name, as it is elsewhere called “the ark of Jehovah” in 1 Chron. 15:3.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance, and the labour they spend on this one phrase is not idle. What they are together establishing is the sheer holiness of the thing David is transporting: it is not a relic or a talisman but the seat of the LORD who dwells between the cherubim, the object upon which the ineffable Name is called, so sacred that a later age would not pronounce that Name aloud. The Chronicler piles the titles up — the ark of God, the LORD enthroned upon the cherubim, the bearer of the Name — precisely, one feels, to prepare the reader for what happens at the threshing-floor; for the disaster of the next verses will turn entirely on the failure to reckon with this holiness, and the weight of these divine names is the measure of how grave a thing it was to handle the ark amiss.
7:
“And they carried the ark of God in a new cart out of the house of Abinadab: and Uzza and Ahio drave the cart.” Here the witnesses divide over the very act that will prove fatal — the placing of the ark upon a cart. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the newness of the cart charitably, as a mark of reverence: it was new lest the ark should be profaned by resting on any carriage that had been put to common uses. On the drivers he notes that all the versions understand “Ahio” as brother or brothers, and that Jarchi, from 2 Samuel 6:3, took Uzza and Ahio to be the sons of Abinadab. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, comparing the fuller text of 2 Samuel 6:3, finds the Chronicler’s account an abridgment that wholly omits the detail of 2 Samuel 6:4, and adds that “Ahio” may with different vowel-points be read “his brothers.”

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry will not let the cart pass as a small thing. Who would have thought, he exclaims, that David would commit such an error as this, to carry the ark upon a cart? Because the Philistines had once so carried it, and a special providence had then driven the cart, as told in 1 Samuel 6:12, David supposed that Israel might do the same. But here Henry lays down the principle the whole disaster illustrates: “we must walk by rule, not by example when it varies from the rule — no, not even by those examples which Providence has owned.” The ark was to be borne upon the shoulders of the Levites, not drawn by oxen; the Philistines’ cart was the expedient of heathen who knew no better, and God’s tolerating it among them was no warrant for his own people to imitate it.

So the two readings of the cart stand side by side, and between them lies the theological nerve of the chapter. Clarke’s charitable eye sees David’s care to honour the ark with a vehicle unused and clean; Henry’s sees the fatal substitution of human contrivance and pious precedent for the plain command of God. They are not simply contradictory — the cart may have been both well-meant and wrong — but the emphasis is opposite, and the sequel decides between them. For it was precisely this method, the ark swaying on a cart behind stumbling oxen rather than steady on the shoulders of its appointed bearers, that would put Uzza’s hand where it should never have been. The witnesses thus set the reader up to understand the coming breach not as an arbitrary stroke but as the fruit of a well-intentioned disregard of the rule.
14:
“And the ark of God remained with the family of Obed-edom in his house three months. And the LORD blessed the house of Obed-edom, and all that he had.” The chapter that opened in disaster ends in blessing, and the witnesses gather round the mercy that rested on the Gittite’s household. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording: “with the family” is literally “by” or near the house, and “in his house” means in its own house or shrine; where the Chronicler writes “the house of Obed-edom, and all that he had,” Samuel has “Obed-edom, and all his house” (compare 2 Samuel 6:12). As to the nature of the blessing, he points to the numerous and vigorous sons recorded of Obed-edom in 1 Chron. 26:4-8, and to the settled peace of the household that trusts in the LORD, as in Psalms 125.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the extravagant gloss of the Targum, which makes the blessing a miracle of fruitfulness: that Obed-edom’s wife and his eight daughters-in-law each bore eight children at a birth, so that in one day there were counted, of fathers and children, fourscore and one. Clarke will not vouch for it — “this exposition will not be generally received,” he says drily, “but all rabbins must be allowed to deal in the marvellous” — yet he lets it stand as witness to how richly the household was thought to have prospered under the ark.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the closing lesson, and sets the blessing deliberately against the death that went before. Let the blessing which the ark brought to the house of Obed-edom, he says, encourage us to welcome God’s ordinances into our own houses, as those who believe the ark to be a guest that nobody shall lose by. Let it not be the less precious to us because to some it proves a stone of stumbling; if the gospel is to some a savour of death unto death, as the ark was to Uzza, let us receive it in the love of it, and it will be to us a savour of life unto life — Henry’s echo of 2 Corinthians 2:16. The witnesses do not divide; they close the chapter on its great reversal. The same ark that had “broken forth” upon a presumptuous hand now pours blessing on a reverent house, and the three months of Obed-edom’s prospering become the pledge and the encouragement that will move David, in the next chapter, to fetch it up at last the right way. Judgment and mercy flow from one and the same holy presence; all turns on how it is received.
8:
“And David and all Israel played before God with all their might, and with singing, and with harps, and with psalteries, and with timbrels, and with cymbals, and with trumpets.” The verse paints the exuberance of the procession, and the witnesses here are few and chiefly text-critical, content largely to let the scene stand. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry it in their running narrative of the ascent, its six kinds of music the instruments with which David and all Israel made their joy before God as the ark went up.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical witness, works over the words and their differences from the parallel in Samuel. “Played,” he says, means rather “were dancing” to the music. The Chronicle’s “with all their might, and with singing” agrees with the Septuagint and the Syriac, whereas the Hebrew of Samuel has the strange expression “with all woods of cypresses” — a reading he judges due to a confusion of similar letters and a transposition, the Greek there rendering it “in strength.” For “cymbals and trumpets” he notes that Samuel’s Hebrew has sistrums, a kind of rattle, and cymbals, the word for sistrum occurring only there, while the Chronicler uses a later term for cymbals. These are not idle observations: they show the Chronicler writing in the vocabulary of a later age, rendering the old worship into the liturgical language of his own day, when the temple music had its settled forms.

Ellicott adds, in his note on the stroke that follows, a word that reaches back to this verse: the phrase “before God” here answers to Samuel’s “by the ark of God” (see his comment at v.10) — for to play “before God” was to play before the ark, the token and seat of his presence. That small equivalence gives the joy its bearing: the singing and the harps and the dancing are not merely a royal festivity but an act of worship directed at the throne of the LORD moving in their midst. The picture the witnesses leave, then, is of a whole nation caught up in music and gladness around the holy thing — a gladness genuine and God-ward, and for that very reason all the more terrible in its sudden reversal, when the joy that filled the road to the threshing-floor would break off in a moment at the death of a single man.
10:
“And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Uzza, and he smote him, because he put his hand to the ark: and there he died before God.” The blow falls, and the witnesses read it as the vindication of God’s holiness. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the Chronicler has abridged Samuel’s “and God smote him there,” and lingers over the difficult ground of the sentence: “because he put his hand to the ark” renders a Hebrew phrase whose counterpart in Samuel is obscure and occurs nowhere else, so that it cannot be told whether the Chronicler preserved the original reading or himself guessed at its sense — the Syriac reads “because he put forth his hand,” the Targum “because he sinned.” “Before God,” he adds, answers to Samuel’s “by the ark of God,” and he compares the stroke upon the men of Beth-shemesh who looked into the ark in 1 Samuel 6:19.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the plain lesson: let the case of Uzza warn us to take heed of presumption or rashness with regard to holy things, and not to think that a right intention will justify a wrong action. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the same truth into the doctrine of a jealous God. The death of Uzza, he says, like that of Nadab and Abihu, proclaims aloud that God will be sanctified in those that come nigh unto him — he cites the sentence of Leviticus 10:3 — and that the nearer any are to him, the more displeased he is with their presumptions. Let the damp this gave to Israel’s joy teach us always to rejoice with trembling, and to serve the LORD with fear even when we serve him with gladness.

On the reason for the stroke the witnesses do not divide: it was the laying of a presumptuous hand upon the holy ark, in disregard of the reverence God’s own presence demands. None of them will allow that Uzza’s good intention altered the case, and none softens the severity into mere misfortune; the anger was real, righteous, and instructive. Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative through the blow to David’s displeasure, letting the bare sequence speak. Yet under the shared verdict lies the finer point the chapter has been building: that a man struck down for touching the ark was a man set in that peril by a whole enterprise conducted off the rule, so that the death of Uzza is at once a personal judgment on a rash hand and a corporate rebuke to a nation that had fetched up its God with more zeal than obedience.
11:
“And David was displeased, because the LORD had made a breach upon Uzza: wherefore that place is called Perez-uzza to this day.” The witnesses take up both the new name and the king’s troubled heart. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains “made a breach” as “broke forth against,” the same verb that recurs when the LORD breaks forth upon the Philistines at 1 Chron. 14:11, and which is used of God’s threatened breaking-out upon a presumptuous people at Exodus 19:22. The naming of the place, “Perez-uzza,” he reads literally as “and one called that place”; and the note “unto this day” he takes to be the Chronicler’s exact transcript of his source, not necessarily a claim that the name still stood in his own later time. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gives the sense of the name — “the breach of Uzza” — and turns at once to the state of David’s spirit.

For the harder question the verse raises is what to make of David’s displeasure. Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry answer it with one voice, almost in the same words. Let David’s displeasure on this occasion, they say, caution us to take heed to our own spirits when we are under divine rebukes, lest, instead of submitting to God, we quarrel with him: if God be angry with us, shall we dare to be angry with him? The displeasure was a fault to be feared, not a mood to be excused — the flaring-up of a man who, checked in a work he had undertaken for God, was tempted to resent the very hand that checked him.

Henry adds the further caution that runs beneath the name Perez-uzza: the stop thus put to the solemnity should teach us not to be driven off from our duty by those providences that are meant only to drive us from our sins. David, he judges, should have gone on with the work notwithstanding the breach made upon Uzza — and had he sought out the right way, so might the breach itself have been made up. The witnesses thus hold two things together without softening either: the breach was God’s righteous act, a real judgment that left its name on the ground for a memorial; and David’s displeasure at it was the natural but perilous recoil of a good man under correction, a warning to every reader to bow rather than to bristle when the LORD reproves. The place would keep the name of the dead man and the divine anger long after the procession had scattered — a standing monument to the cost of handling holy things amiss.
12:
“And David was afraid of God that day, saying, How shall I bring the ark of God home to me?” With the breach fresh before him, David’s displeasure gives way to fear, and the question that had seemed so simple becomes a burden. The witnesses fall almost silent here; only the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers, and his interest is chiefly in the words of the text. He notes first the Chronicler’s reverent habit with the divine names: where the verse and its neighbours say “God” and “the ark of God” — here, and again at v.8 and v.14 — the parallel in Samuel has “Jehovah,” the Chronicler or his authority avoiding the frequent use of that most holy Name. The same scruple that would later shrink from pronouncing the Name aloud is at work in the very telling of the story.

Ellicott marks two further turns of the language. “Saying” stands where Samuel has “and said”; and the word rendered “How” is hek, an Aramaic form, which he thinks may be due to a transcriber rather than to the author himself — a small late-comer lodged in the text. The phrase “How shall I bring” answers to Samuel’s “how shall the ark come,” two different voices of the same verb, the one throwing the action upon David, the other leaving it, as it were, to the ark’s own coming.

Slight as these notes are, they open the verse’s inner movement. The fear of God that seizes David “that day” is the fitting recoil from the displeasure of the verse before: the man who had been vexed that the LORD made a breach is brought, on the same day, to a holy dread of the God whose presence he had handled so lightly. And the question he asks — “How shall I bring the ark home to me?” — is the honest confession of a king who has learned, at the cost of Uzza’s life, that the ark cannot be fetched up by good intentions and a new cart, but must be approached in a manner worthy of the Name that is called upon it. The Chronicler’s careful substitution of “God” for the ineffable Name only underscores the point his narrative is making: that David’s whole enterprise has run aground on the holiness of the One he sought to honour, and that the way forward, hidden from him in this moment of fear, will have to be a way of obedience he has not yet found. The verse leaves him arrested — afraid, questioning, and for the present unable to go on.
13:
“So David brought not the ark home to himself to the city of David, but carried it aside into the house of Obed-edom the Gittite.” The witnesses here are drawn to a single figure — the man in whose house the ark found lodging — and to the puzzle of his name. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening more exactly, “And David caused not the ark to turn aside unto himself,” a different verb from the “bring home” of the verse before; the king, checked by fear, diverts the ark from its intended path into a nearer resting-place. Then he takes up “Obed-edom the Gittite.” Since 1 Chron. 26:1-4 makes Obed-edom a Korhite Levite, the term “Gittite” is generally understood to mean a native of Gath-rimmon, a Levitical town assigned in Joshua 21:24 to the great clan of Kohath — the very clan charged with the carriage of the ark, of which Obed-edom was a member, according to Numbers 4:15.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reaches the same conclusion — that Obed-edom was a Levite, not a Philistine — but explains the name “Gittite” by another route. He was, Clarke holds, only a sojourner at Gath, and thence called Gittite, though originally a Levite, as is plain from the lists of 1 Chron. 15:17-18. So the witnesses agree on the man and differ on his surname: was he a Levite who had dwelt among the Philistines of Gath, or a native of the Levitical Gath-rimmon? The point is small, but it settles a real question — whether the ark was housed with a foreigner or, as both finally allow, with a Levite of the appointed clan.

And that agreement carries a quiet irony the witnesses’ notes bring to light. The ark had just struck down Uzza for the unlawful touch of a hand that had no warrant to bear it; now it is carried aside — “turned aside,” in Ellicott’s phrase — into the house of a man of Kohath, the very family that Numbers 4:15 appointed to carry the holy things upon their shoulders. What the whole solemn procession, with its new cart and its thirty thousand, had failed to do rightly, providence now accomplishes almost by accident: the ark comes to rest in the keeping of one of its lawful bearers. David, afraid and baffled, does not yet see the lesson written in the address to which his fear has driven the ark; but the reader, guided by the witnesses, can see that the God who would not be served by the wrong hands has quietly brought his throne into the house of the right ones.
